abstract:
This study presents an architectural and analytical investigation into the identity and spatial characteristics of Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa, challenging the historical claims regarding the existence of an "alleged temple" at the site. The first part of the research focuses on the orientation and geometric properties of the Noble Sanctuary (Al-Haram Al-Qudsi). Utilizing satellite imagery and software analysis, the author demonstrates that the primary axis of the mosque is oriented toward Makkah. Furthermore, a comparative geometric analysis reveals a striking symmetry between Al-Masjid Al-Aqsa and the Ka`bah; both structures utilize a rare trapezoidal plan and adhere to the "golden ratio" in their proportions. These findings suggest a deep-rooted material and spiritual bond between the two holiest Islamic sites that predates the Muslim conquest of Jerusalem.

The second part of the paper scrutinizes descriptions of the temple found in the Old Testament from an engineering and structural perspective. The analysis highlights significant internal contradictions within biblical texts concerning the number of laborers, the dimensions of the porch, the height of the bronze pillars, and the capacity of the sacrificial "sea." Additionally, the research notes that the architectural specifications provided in the text—such as the use of square, unhewn stones—diverge sharply from the archaeological reality of the rectangular, dressed stones found at the foundations of the Western (Al-Buraq) Wall. By documenting these mathematical and structural inconsistencies, the study argues that the alleged temple lacks a consistent architectural basis and concludes that the historical record supports the Islamic tradition of Prophet Solomon renewing a mosque rather than constructing a temple.
